elements.sent-2411-012: To address these challenges and achieve efficiency, scalability, and generalisation across diverse real-world networks simultaneously, we propose combining the computational efficiency of traditional graph methods with the holistic nature of GNNs.

elements.sent-2411-014: To this end, we propose Graph-aware Logistic Regression (GLR), a non-neural model that combines information from various aspects of the graph through a simple yet efficient learning process.

elements.section-2411-004: Graph-aware Logistic Regression: a Simple Non-Neural Model
elements.sent-2411-065: In this section, we introduce Graph-aware Logistic Regression (GLR), our proposed non-neural approach for node classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-066: GLR builds upon a logistic regression model that leverages the entire set of information available in attributed graphs; the topology of the graph as well as the node features (see Figure~\ref{fig:glr for an illustration).}
elements.sent-2411-067: Through this setting, GLR is able to adapt to several kinds of graph, by balancing weights between graph structure and feature information.
elements.sent-2411-068: Thanks to its simple architecture, it does not require extensive hyperparameter optimisation and achieves high scalability compared to GNN-based approaches.
elements.sent-2411-069: Traditional logistic regression models applied to the graph field typically computes the class probability $\hat{y _{u}$ of a node $u$ using its corresponding feature vector $X_u$: \begin{equation}\hat{y}_{u} = \text{softmax}(w^{T}X_{u} + b)\end{equation} However, such model completely disregards the structure of the graph, which contains essential information in situations such as strong label homophily among nodes.}
elements.sent-2411-070: An alternative approach involves fitting a logistic regression model directly on the topology of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-071: For instance, one of the logistic regression-based LINK model~\cite{zheleva2009join computes the class probability of a node $u$ using its corresponding row in the adjacency matrix $A_u$, in other words a binary vector representing its neighbourhood: \begin{equation}\hat{y}_{u} = \text{softmax}(w^{T}A_{u} + b)\end{equation} Despite good results on social networks where label homophily is relatively low, LINK completely disregards node features, which prove to be essential in specific real-world networks (see Section~\ref{sec:Results ).}}
elements.sent-2411-072: We build GLR upon the combination of the two previous principles, and develop a logistic regression model relying on both the graph topology and the node attributes.
elements.sent-2411-073: For this purpose, we compute the initial vector representation $h_u$ of each node $u$ by concatenating its neighbourhood representation from the adjacency matrix $A_u$, with its initial feature vector $X_u$.
elements.sent-2411-074: Then, we feed node $u$'s concatenated representation to a logistic regression model.
elements.sent-2411-075: More formally, GLR uses a mapping in the form: \begin{equation}\begin{split} h_{u} = CONCAT(A_{u}, X_{u}) \hat{y}_{u} = \text{softmax}(w^{T}h_{u} + b) \end{split}\end{equation} where $CONCAT$ denotes a concatenation operation, and where $w$ and $b$ are learnable weights and biases.
elements.sent-2411-076: The intuition behind GLR is straightforward; the model learns to classify a node leveraging information from both its connectivity within the graph and the features it holds, therefore, taking advantage of all the information available in the graph, in a similar way to GNNs.
elements.sent-2411-077: However, unlike the message passing scheme, the proposed architecture does not involves signal aggregation from direct neighbourhood.
elements.sent-2411-078: While such signal aggregation can be desirable in homophilous graphs, it may introduce uninformative and noisy representations in heterophilous settings.
elements.sent-2411-079: We argue that augmenting a node's feature vector with its neighbourhood representation allows for more flexible adjustment during the learning process: higher weights will be given to the graph structure representation in the presence of strong homophily, and conversely, more emphasis will be placed on the node's features in the presence of strong heterophily, leading to better generalisation ability.
elements.sent-2411-080: Additionally, our approach has benefits considering computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-081: In GLR, the training time complexity is in $\mathcal{O (n(n+L))$, where $n+L$ corresponds to the number of parameters to learn.}
elements.sent-2411-082: In GNNs, one training pass of a single-layer network induces an $\mathcal{O (|E|L + nLd)$ cost, with $d$ the size of the hidden dimension; $|E|+L$ induced by the feature aggregation and $nLd$ induced by the weight matrix multiplication.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Therefore, our approach becomes highly efficient for real-world networks where nodes hold large feature vectors containing, for instance, bag-of-words representations of textual content (as for Wikipedia-based networks).
elements.section-2411-005: Limits of the Current Evaluation Procedures
elements.sent-2411-084: In this section, we identify the practice patterns emerging from the rapid expansion of the GNN field, and that could potentially mislead the readers in how they measure progress.
elements.sent-2411-085: Training GNN models involves various choices for the experimental framework, such as the number of nodes in the train-test splits, the selection of these nodes, the features used, hyperparameter values, the distribution of labels per class, etc. Even though authors make efforts to adhere to previous recommendations for these parameters, it is common that small variations occur from one framework to the next.
elements.sent-2411-086: This can make it challenging to distinguish the progress achieved by a specific model architecture from changes in the experimental setup.
elements.sent-2411-087: To illustrate this, authors in~\cite{shchur_pitfalls_2019 emphasis how modifications in the choice of training and test splits could lead to drastic changes in model rankings.}
elements.sent-2411-088: Numerous works mention the limited number of datasets used to assess GNN performance~\cite{hu2020open,palowitch_graphworld_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-089: Since the introduction of foundational models like GCN~\cite{kipf2017semisupervised , evaluation has predominantly relied on three well-known citation networks, namely Cora, Pubmed and Citeseer~\cite{yang2016revisiting .}}
elements.sent-2411-090: This practice is justified regarding the challenges and time investment associated with creating new datasets.
elements.sent-2411-091: Moreover, maintaining continuity in the evaluation process requires consistent dataset choices for fair comparisons between older and recent approaches.
elements.sent-2411-092: However, over time, this practice reveals drawbacks, particularly the risk of overfitting to datasets~\cite{palowitch_graphworld_2022 , especially when the same subset of nodes is used for training.}
elements.sent-2411-093: Moreover, the lack of graph diversity can overshadow the limitations of the evaluated models in terms of generalisation to more diverse real-world data~\cite{lipton2018troubling,ferrari_dacrema_are_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-094: This trend has been observed with the aforementioned citation networks~\cite{zhu2020beyond,lim2021new ; there is a strong assumption that GNNs are highly effective on these datasets since their aggregation scheme design perfectly fits the high degree of label homophily these networks exhibit~\cite{zhu2020beyond,maekawa2022beyond,palowitch_graphworld_2022 .}}
elements.subsection-2411-009: Lack of non-neural models in benchmarks
elements.sent-2411-095: The early success of GNNs in outperforming non-neural baselines established them as new benchmarks~\cite{kipf2017semisupervised,velivckovic2017graph .}
elements.sent-2411-096: This phenomenon may explain why current GNN evaluation frameworks seldom encompass comparisons with models beyond other GNN architectures or MLPs.
elements.sent-2411-097: Moreover, when considered, non-neural baselines are often developed in their traditional form; topological heuristics, such as diffusion models, do not leverage node features and feature-based approaches, such as logistic regression, completely discard graph topology.
elements.sent-2411-098: We argue that these characteristics hinder non-neural models from emerging as strong competitors against GNN approaches.
elements.sent-2411-099: In this section, we detail our proposition to overcome current GNN evaluation framework limitations, and give information about both neural and non-neural models included in our benchmark.

elements.sent-2411-101: To address one of the main pitfalls of GNN evaluation, i.e. the change of evaluation protocols over time, we build a unified framework in which we test all models.
elements.sent-2411-102: In order to avoid the influence of train-test split choice on model performance~\cite{shchur_pitfalls_2019 , we apply $k$-fold cross validation, i.e. we use $k$ different training-test splits to estimate the performance of a model.}
elements.sent-2411-103: We build each fold in a stratified custom, ensuring that class proportions are maintained.
elements.sent-2411-104: Our procedure results in a total of $k$ training and testing experiments for each model and dataset.
elements.sent-2411-105: Moreover, to mitigate the potential impact of random initialisation, which can occur with GNN models, we conduct each fold experiment three times and use the average result.
elements.sent-2411-106: Then, we average test performance across all repetitions and folds to determine the model performance.
elements.sent-2411-107: Finally, we ensure fair comparison between approaches by relying on fixed seeds to define the $k$ folds for all models.

elements.sent-2411-225: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and their message passing framework that leverages both structural and feature information, have become a standard method for solving graph-based machine learning problems.
elements.sent-2411-226: However, these approaches still struggle to generalise well beyond datasets that exhibit strong homophily, where nodes of the same class tend to connect.
elements.sent-2411-227: This limitation has led to the development of complex neural architectures that pose challenges in terms of efficiency and scalability.
elements.sent-2411-228: In response to these limitations, we focus on simpler and more scalable approaches and introduce Graph-aware Logistic Regression (GLR), a non-neural model designed for node classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-229: Unlike traditional graph algorithms that use only a fraction of the information accessible to GNNs, our proposed model simultaneously leverages both node features and the relationships between entities.
elements.sent-2411-230: However instead of relying on message passing, our approach encodes each node's relationships as an additional feature vector, which is then combined with the node's self attributes.
elements.sent-2411-231: Extensive experimental results, conducted within a rigorous evaluation framework, show that our proposed GLR approach outperforms both foundational and sophisticated state-of-the-art GNN models in node classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-232: The recent interest in Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) has led to the emergence of a huge amount of novel approaches to tackle different downstream machine learning tasks on relational data, such as node classification, graph classification, or link prediction~\citep{gasteiger2018predict,xu2018representation,wu2019simplifying,chen2020simple,zhu2020beyond,wu2020comprehensive .}
elements.sent-2411-233: Because they can learn complex representations by leveraging elements from both the graph structure and the node attributes, these approaches have been widely adopted for real-world network analysis, where information spans across multiple dimensions~\citep{zhou2020graph .}
elements.sent-2411-234: However, recent research has shown that GNNs struggle to generalise well across datasets with diverse characteristics~\citep{zhu2020beyond,li2021beyond,maekawa2022beyond .}
elements.sent-2411-235: In particular, extending beyond networks exhibiting strong homophily, where nodes with similar labels tend to connect, remains challenging and has led to the design of complex model architectures~\citep{zhu2020beyond,du22gbk,ma22meta,wang2023label,wu2023learning .}
elements.sent-2411-236: These sophisticated neural approaches result in higher computational complexity and an increased number of hyperparameter choices.
elements.sent-2411-237: Despite efforts to reduce the complexity of GNNs~\citep{wu2019simplifying,he2020lightgcn,lim2021large,mao2021ultragcn , neural approaches still struggle to address both scalability and generalisability challenges simultaneously.}
elements.sent-2411-238: In contrast, traditional graph algorithms, such as diffusion models~\citep{zhu2005semi and linear classifiers~\citep{zheleva2009join , offer advantages in terms of simplicity and efficiency.}}
elements.sent-2411-239: However, their overly simplistic architecture typically harness only a fraction of the information accessible to GNNs, focusing solely on either the graph topology or the node attributes, but not both.
elements.sent-2411-240: This limitation prevents them for being strong competitor to neural approaches, causing researchers to overlook them in recent benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-241: In this work, propose combining the computational efficiency of traditional graph methods with the holistic capabilities of GNNs to address the node classification task.
elements.sent-2411-242: This requires bridging the gap in the information accessible to both approaches.
elements.sent-2411-243: To this end, we introduce Graph-aware Logistic Regression (GLR), a non-neural model that combines information from various aspects of the graph through a simple yet efficient learning process for solving node classification problems.
elements.sent-2411-244: Specifically, GLR represents each node's neighbourhood within the original graph as a feature vector, which is then combined with the node's original attributes before being fed into a simple non-neural model, as illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:glr .}
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{img/node_classif_model_coloured.pdf} \caption{Overview of our Graph-aware Logistic Regression (GLR) method for node classification. We consider both the topological and attribute information by concatenating ($||$) the graph adjacency and feature matrices. We then feed the result into a logistic regression $f$.} \label{fig:glr}
elements.sent-2411-245: We mitigate common pitfalls in GNN evaluation~\citep{shchur_pitfalls_2019,zhu2020beyond,you2020design,platonov2023critical , such as limited diversity in benchmark network characteristics and the absence of non-neural baselines, through careful selection of datasets that vary widely in size, density, and homophily.}
elements.sent-2411-246: Additionally, we explore a range of non-neural baselines to compare against GNNs.
elements.sent-2411-247: Through extensive experiments conducted under this setting, we show that GLR outperforms both foundational and sophisticated GNNs while achieving higher generalisation ability.
elements.sent-2411-248: Moreover, our approach reduces computation time reduction up to two orders of magnitude compared to its best neural competitor.
elements.sent-2411-249: Finally, we conduct an in-depth analysis of performance by examining the graph homophily property.
elements.sent-2411-250: We show that the commonly used label homophily, is insufficient for explaining GNN performance.
elements.sent-2411-251: To address this, we introduce feature homophily, which assesses the similarity of connected nodes based on their attributes.
elements.sent-2411-252: We discuss how this property influences performance and emphasise the limitations of GNNs in effectively leveraging node attributes when sufficiently informative.
elements.sent-2411-253: Overall, our contributions encompass the following key aspects:.
elements.sent-2411-254: We introduce Graph-aware Logistic Regression, a simple non-neural model in which we consider both the graph topology and the node attributes as features to address node classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-255: We show that, within a rigorous evaluation framework, our proposed approach outperforms GNNs while achieving better generalisation ability.
elements.sent-2411-256: We briefly review some representative neural and non-neural models to address node classification.
elements.sent-2411-257: Early attempts to consider both the node features and the relational information led to iterative methods, which involved handcrafting features to capture link patterns, such as node degree or the frequency of specific labels among connected nodes~\citep{neville2000iterative,macskassy2003simple .}
elements.sent-2411-258: However, the heavy reliance of these approaches on heuristics for feature design limits their generalisation ability.
elements.sent-2411-259: In contrast, random walk-based methods avoid these heuristics by exploiting spectral properties of matrix representation of graphs.
elements.sent-2411-260: Approaches like the diffusion model~\citep{zhu2005semi , label propagation~\citep{raghavan2007near , or PageRank-based classifier~\citep{lin2010semi have proven highly effective while requiring limited computational resources.}}}

elements.sent-2411-263: The $k$-hops neighbourhood aggregation mechanism common to these models is often considered as a smoothing operation over node signals~\citep{li2018deeper,liu2021graph , which explains GNN performance gains over fully connected networks; since smoothing makes the features similar, connected nodes end up with similar representations.}
elements.sent-2411-264: Therefore, recent works show how homophilous datasets favour GNN designs, such as the ones frequently used in benchmarks, and that performance is dropping as soon as networks are showing heterophily~\citep{zhu2020beyond,lim2021new .}

elements.sent-2411-276: We consider an attributed graph as a tuple $G=(V,E,X)$, where $V$ is a set of vertices, $E \subseteq V \times V$ is a set of edges and $X^{|V|\times L}$ an attribute matrix assigning $L$ numerical attributes to each node in the graph.

elements.subsection-2411-024: Graph Neural Networks and message passing
elements.sent-2411-279: GNNs aim to learn low-dimensional space representations $h_u \in \mathbb{R ^{w}$, with $w \ll L$ for each node $u$ in a graph.}
elements.sent-2411-280: By encoding the similarities between the nodes, these representations should support the use of downstream machine learning tasks, e.g., node classification, graph classification or link prediction.
elements.sent-2411-281: One of the main advantages of GNNs is the use of both the graph structure and the feature matrix to compute these representations; at each layer $l$, a node representation $h_{u ^{l}$ is computed via a message passing scheme, which aggregates feature information from the direct neighbourhood of the node.}
elements.sent-2411-282: Most of the recent GNN models are thus derived from the following node update rule: \begin{equation}h_{N(u)}^{l} = \phi(\{h_v^{l-1}, \forall v \in N(u) \}) h_u^{l} = \psi\left(h_{u}^{l-1}, h_{N(u)}^{l}\right)\end{equation} where $\mathcal{N (u)$ denotes node $u$'s neighbourhood, $\phi$ is an aggregation function (e.g., average) and $\psi$ is an update function (e.g., sum).}
elements.sent-2411-283: Several works emphasise how GNN's message passing scheme is inherently built upon a strong homophily assumption, hence the great performance of these models on networks with such characteristics~\citep{zhu2020beyond,maekawa2022beyond,palowitch_graphworld_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-284: Formally, label homophily $\mathcal{H _l(u)$ of a node $u$ is the proportion of its neighbourhood sharing its label~\citep{du22gbk : $\mathcal{H _l(u) = \frac{1 {d_u \sum_{v \in \mathcal{N (u) \mathds{1 (y_u = y_v)$, where $d_u$ is the degree of node $u$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-285: GLR builds upon a logistic regression model that leverages the entire set of information available in attributed graphs; the topology of the graph as well as the node features, as illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:glr .}
elements.sent-2411-286: By balancing weights between graph structure and feature information, GLR is able to adapt to several kinds of graphs.
elements.subsection-2411-026: Naive logistic regression models for node classification
elements.sent-2411-287: A naive approach to apply logistic regression to node classification is to disregard the graph topology and only consider the node features.
elements.sent-2411-288: Under this setting, the class probability $\hat{y _{u}$ of a node $u$ is computed using its corresponding feature vector $X_u$: \begin{equation}\hat{y}_{u} = \text{softmax}(\beta^{T}X_{u} + \beta_0)\end{equation} where $\beta_0$ and $\beta$ are learnable parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-289: This approach treats the nodes as independent from each other, resulting in a significant loss of information.
elements.sent-2411-290: In particular, in the case of label homophilous graphs, this overlooked information is valuable and may greatly improve prediction accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-291: An alternative approach involves fitting a logistic regression models directly on the topology of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-292: For instance, one of the logistic regression-based LINK model~\citep{zheleva2009join computes the class probability of a node $u$ using its corresponding row in the adjacency matrix $A_u$, in other words a binary vector representing its neighbourhood: \begin{equation}\hat{y}_{u} = \text{softmax}(\beta^{T}A_{u} + \beta_0)\end{equation} However, this simple baseline fails to leverage node features when they are sufficiently informative.}
elements.sent-2411-293: In particular, we show in Section~\ref{sec:Results that, unlike commonly used datasets in the current GNN literature, such as Cora, Pubmed and Citeseer, other networks may exhibit a strong correlation between node feature similarity and node connectivity.}
elements.sent-2411-294: In such cases, overlooking the node features represents a major loss of information, potentially negatively affecting overall performance and generalisation ability.
elements.sent-2411-295: More formally, GLR predicts the class probability $\hat{y _{u}$ using a mapping in the form: \begin{equation}\begin{split} h_{u} = CONCAT(A_{u}, X_{u}) \hat{y}_{u} = \text{softmax}(\beta^{T}h_{u} + \beta_0) \end{split}\end{equation} where $CONCAT$ denotes a concatenation operation.}
elements.sent-2411-296: We argue that augmenting a node's feature vector with its neighbourhood representation allows for more flexible adjustment during the learning process: in the presence of strong homophily, our model may gives more importance to the neighbourhood structure, and conversely, more emphasis will be placed on the node's features in the presence of strong heterophily.
elements.sent-2411-297: With GLR, the training time complexity is in $\mathcal{O (n(n+L))$, where $n+L$ corresponds to the number of parameters to learn.}
elements.sent-2411-298: In GNNs, one training pass of a single-layer network induces an $\mathcal{O (mL + nLd)$ cost, with $d$ the size of the hidden dimension; $m+L$ induced by the feature aggregation and $nLd$ induced by the weight matrix multiplication.}
elements.sent-2411-299: Therefore, our approach becomes highly efficient for real-world networks where nodes hold large feature vectors containing, for instance, bag-of-words representations of textual content (as for Wikipedia-based networks).. Relationship to SGC.
elements.sent-2411-300: Our logistic regression based approach is closely related to the SGC model~\citep{wu2019simplifying .}
elements.sent-2411-301: SGC implements a GNN where the non-linearities have been removed.
elements.sent-2411-302: Therefore, the class prediction $\hat Y$ for $n$ nodes is obtained through a $l$-layer GNN in the form: \begin{equation}\hat Y_{SGC} = softmax(S \cdots SSX\Theta^{(1)}\Theta^{(2)} \cdots \Theta^{(l)}) \Leftrightarrow \hat Y_{SGC} = softmax(S^{l}X\Theta)\end{equation} where $S$ denotes the normalized adjacency matrix of the graph, and $\Theta = \Theta^{(1)}\Theta^{(2) \cdots \Theta^{(l)}$ is the reparametrised weight matrix.}
elements.sent-2411-303: Consequently, the SGC model is a logistic regression model in which the input matrix $S^{l X$ is obtained through a preprocessing step corresponding to signal aggregation.}

elements.sent-2411-368: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have become a standard method for solving graph-based machine learning problems.

We then feed the result into a logistic regression $f$.} \label{fig:glr}
elements.sent-2411-369: The recent interest in Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) led to the emergence of a huge amount of novel approaches to tackle different downstream machine learning tasks on relational data, such as node classification, graph classification, or link prediction~\cite{wu2020comprehensive .}
elements.sent-2411-370: Because they can learn complex representations by leveraging elements from both the graph structure and attributes, these approaches have been widely adopted for real-world network analysis, where information spans across multiple dimensions~\cite{zhou2020graph .}
elements.sent-2411-371: However, despite promising performance, the search for increasingly sophisticated GNN architecture designs present major challenges in terms of generalisability~\cite{zhu2020beyond,li2021beyond,maekawa2022beyond ~-- for instance considering both homophilous and hetereohpilous networks~-- and scalability~\cite{he2020lightgcn,mao2021ultragcn .}}
elements.sent-2411-372: To this end, some research efforts have aimed to simplify GNN designs while maintaining performance~\cite{wu2019simplifying,he2020lightgcn,lim2021large,mao2021ultragcn .}
elements.sent-2411-373: Although these simplified approaches regularly achieve competitive results compared to complex models, they still require the training of multi-layer neural networks, reducing efficiency, and often fail to address generalisability issues.
elements.sent-2411-374: To tackle these challenges, we question the necessity of neural approaches to simultaneously achieve efficiency, scalability, and generalisation across diverse real-world networks.
elements.sent-2411-375: Therefore, we shift our focus to non-neural methods for solving node classification tasks in graphs.
elements.sent-2411-376: We aim to bridge the performance gap between traditional non-neural models and GNNs, by addressing the discrepancy in the information accessible to both classes of approaches.
elements.sent-2411-377: Indeed, traditional non-neural models typically leverage only a fraction of the information used by GNNs, focusing either on the topology of the graph or the node features, but not both.
elements.sent-2411-378: We argue that this information disparity contributes to make the traditional approaches weak competitors to GNNs, potentially underestimating their capabilities and obscuring the actual progress achieved by neural methods~\cite{ferrari_dacrema_are_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-379: Therefore, we propose Graph-aware Logistic Regression (GLR), a non-neural model that combines information from various aspects of the graph through a simple yet efficient learning process.
elements.sent-2411-380: We introduce Graph-aware Logistic Regression (GLR), a simple non-neural model in which we consider both the graph topology and the node attributes as features to address node classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-381: We show that, within a rigorous evaluation framework, the proposed approach outperforms GNNs while achieving better generalisation ability.
elements.sent-2411-382: In this section, we briefly review some representative GNNs as well as attempts to simplify their designs.

elements.sent-2411-385: In contrast with this line of work, a few attempts have been made to design simpler neural architectures.

elements.sent-2411-387: Similarly, LightGCN~\cite{he2020lightgcn removes non-linear activation and feature transformation to produce a scalable model for collaborative filtering tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-388: UltraGCN~\cite{mao2021ultragcn goes one step further and removes explicit message passing and replace it with an approximation of the limit of infinite-layers graph convolutions.}
elements.sent-2411-389: LINKX~\cite{lim2021large uses a series of Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) to separately represent the graph and feature matrices and perform predictions.}

elements.sent-2411-441: In this work, we have proposed a simple and scalable non-neural model, GLR, that leverages information from both the structure and the features of the graph to solve node classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-442: Extended experiments conducted within a rigorous evaluation framework have shown that GLR outperforms both traditional graph algorithms and GNNs on the majority of evaluated datasets.
elements.sent-2411-443: Additionally, across diverse graph characteristics, including homophily, we showed GLR's high generalisation ability.
elements.sent-2411-444: Finally, GLR achieves superior results without sacrificing performance for computation time, demonstrating a two-order-of-magnitude improvement in computation time compared to the best GNNs.
elements.sent-2411-445: To gain insights into our results, we have investigated the graph homophily property by dividing it into two components: similarities in node labels and similarities in node features.
elements.sent-2411-446: We showed that, in contrast to the proposed approach, most of the recent GNN architectures struggled to leverage information from features when it was sufficiently available.
elements.sent-2411-447: Overall, our work shows that non-neural approaches, when leveraging sufficient information from the graph, despite their simplicity, can achieve comparable or higher performance than GNNs.
elements.sent-2411-448: Despite great results, GLR still has room for improvements as performance gains against GNNs remain minor in some cases.
elements.sent-2411-449: Additionally, our analyses are limited to node classification task as well as some of the most frequently used GNN architectures and datasets.
elements.sent-2411-450: For future work we plan to extend this study to other well-know graph-related tasks, such as graph classification and link prediction.. \bibliographystyle{unsrtnat .}
